title: National Sovereignty (Government View, April 2023)
purpose: Where sovereignty is industrialised infrastructure and where it is still contested and fragile.

components.name: Supranational
components.rationale: UN, G7/G20, WTO, EU, NATO are well-established institutions, but their authority vs national interest is under active re-contestation (Russia, China, trade wars). Sits at the edge of Custom-Built.

components.name: Society
components.rationale: Permanent beneficiary; the people a government must serve. Always at the top of the chain.

components.name: Government
components.rationale: The mapping actor — the modern nation-state executive is a mature institutional form though varies substantially in effectiveness.

components.name: Corporations & Shareholders
components.rationale: Capital-market actors operate through highly standardised corporate, securities, and accounting frameworks (IFRS, GAAP, MiFID, SEC); interchangeable across jurisdictions.

components.name: Legitimacy
components.rationale: A government's legitimacy is a judgement made by citizens and allies; it is recognisable but bespoke per regime, no off-the-shelf 'legitimacy product'. Under more pressure in April 2023 than in any recent decade.

components.name: Perception of Success
components.rationale: Narrative construction around outcomes — inflation, immigration, COVID response, Ukraine — is bespoke to each government and media cycle; no standardised measure is authoritative.

components.name: Public Belonging
components.rationale: Social cohesion and felt membership are fragmenting along political, cultural, and digital lines in April 2023; no stable template exists. Trending leftward (more contested), not rightward.

components.name: Election Legitimacy
components.rationale: In mature democracies, elections are procedurally productised (voter rolls, ballots, counting, ODIHR/OAS observation). But perceived legitimacy has been dragged leftward into Custom-Built by 2020 US contestation, 2021 Jan 6, and industrial-scale disinformation. Re-contested across the West.

components.name: Territorial Sovereignty
components.rationale: Westphalian borders, territorial jurisdiction, and military control are the oldest and most codified sovereignty pillar. Well-defined in international law and state practice.

components.name: Economic Sovereignty
components.rationale: Fiscal, monetary, tax, and trade instruments are well-understood institutional products (central banks, treasuries, customs). Re-contested post-2008 and by the 2022-2023 sanctions regime, but still productised.

components.name: Security Sovereignty
components.rationale: Defence procurement, alliance structures (NATO, AUKUS), intelligence services, and military doctrine are mature. Vendor markets exist (primes, PMCs). Still Product rather than Commodity because alliance reconfiguration is bespoke per era.

components.name: Political Sovereignty
components.rationale: Parliamentary/presidential institutions, civil service, judicial independence are established products. But sits below Economic because populist pressures and democratic backsliding have made it more contested in April 2023.

components.name: Cultural Sovereignty
components.rationale: Language, historical narrative, shared myth-making, national identity — deeply bespoke per state, no off-the-shelf answer; increasingly contested by global platforms and diaspora communication.

components.name: Digital Sovereignty
components.rationale: Emerging concept with no settled definition — EU (GAIA-X, DMA/DSA, Data Act), China (Great Firewall, data localisation), US (hyperscaler dominance), India (data residency) are pursuing incompatible visions. Strong inertia from globally-scaled US hyperscaler dependency.

components.name: Land
components.rationale: Physical territory, border control, land registry, conventional military operations — industrialised for centuries. Ukraine war is a reminder of land's primacy, not a change in its evolution stage.

components.name: CNI
components.rationale: Critical National Infrastructure (energy, water, telecoms, finance, health, transport) is regulated under explicit frameworks (UK NIS, EU NIS2, US PPD-21). Vendor markets and standards exist; still Product because OT/IT convergence is uneven.

components.name: Supply Chain
components.rationale: Globalised just-in-time supply chains were industrialised, but COVID, Ukraine, and Taiwan/semiconductor stress have re-opened JIT vs resilience as a bespoke per-sector judgement call. Strong inertia from 30 years of JIT-optimised contracts and tooling.

components.name: Cyber
components.rationale: Cyber defence is a rapidly evolving attacker-defender contest. Frameworks exist (NIST CSF, MITRE ATT&CK, Zero Trust) but state-grade capability, attribution, and offensive cyber are still bespoke per nation.

components.name: Integrity
components.rationale: Anti-corruption institutions exist (auditors, ombudsmen, ICAC/GRECO), but perceived integrity is judged through local culture and is actively contested in populist politics.

components.name: Competency
components.rationale: Professional civil service, public management, measurable delivery KPIs are widespread (NAO, GAO, OECD SIGMA). Recognisable product, though quality varies enormously between states.

components.name: Fairness
components.rationale: Procedural and distributive fairness norms vary by political tradition; no single canonical definition. Actively contested in tax, immigration, and welfare debates.

components.name: Benevolence
components.rationale: The sense that government acts in public rather than private interest — inherently judgemental and culturally specific. Corroded by post-2016 populism and conspiracy-adjacent media.

components.name: Identity
components.rationale: Civic identity is fragmenting along digital, diaspora, and culture-war lines; there is no stable operational model for shared national identity in 2023. Trending earlier in stage, not later.

components.name: Territorial Awareness
components.rationale: Satellite imagery (commercial + state), AIS, land registry, border surveillance, GIS — utility-priced in 2023 (Planet, Maxar, Sentinel) with standardised APIs. Industrialised.

components.name: Crisis Response
components.rationale: Post-COVID, emergency powers, COBR/NSC-style structures, reasonable-worst-case planning, and vaccine/PPE procurement have been codified. Still Product (not Commodity) because each crisis rewrites the playbook.

components.name: Supply-Chain Awareness
components.rationale: Multi-tier supplier visibility is an active investment area (project44, Interos, Everstream, Altana) but deployments are still bespoke systems-integration projects. Moving rightward fast.

components.name: Digital-Chain Awareness
components.rationale: Software bill-of-materials (SBOM), cloud dependency mapping, firmware provenance — nascent. CISA, NCSC, ENISA are publishing guidance but tooling and practice are genesis. SolarWinds & Log4Shell put this on the map.

components.name: Capability Layer
components.rationale: The deliverable machinery of government (agencies, digital services, procurement, civil service, GDS/USDS-style units). Widely recognised product but quality varies; OECD benchmarks exist.

components.name: Laws
components.rationale: Domestic statutes, regulations, case law, drafting, and enforcement are fully codified institutions in functioning states. Commodity infrastructure of the state.

components.name: International Law
components.rationale: Treaty systems, UN Charter, Vienna Conventions, ICJ/ICC, WTO DSB exist and are productised. Enforcement is contested (Russia/Ukraine, US-China) which keeps it mid-Product not Commodity.

components.name: Mainstream Media
components.rationale: Broadcast, print, and cable news are mature commoditised industries with declining margins; standardised distribution. Influence is waning but infrastructure is industrialised.

components.name: Education
components.rationale: State education systems are institutional products with well-defined curricula, teacher training, and accreditation. Vendor markets for ed-tech exist. Culture-war contestation but infrastructure is productised.

components.name: Social Media
components.rationale: Meta, YouTube, TikTok, X operate at commodity scale with utility-like pricing and APIs. Near-monopoly attention infrastructure; the state does not control this layer.

components.name: Art
components.rationale: Creative industries (film, TV, music, games, publishing) are productised global markets with funding bodies, IP regimes, and distribution platforms. Soft-power component.

components.name: Propaganda
components.rationale: Strategic communication, influence operations, and hostile-state information warfare. The craft is old, but 2020s forms (bot networks, deepfakes, algorithmic amplification) are still being built. Bespoke per actor.

annotations.text: Digital sovereignty: genesis/custom; EU-US-China divergence, strong industry inertia
Supply chain re-assessment wave post-COVID & Ukraine; JIT vs JIC re-opening
Contested zone: election legitimacy dragged leftward by 2020-2021 disinformation wave
Industrialised: Westphalian land, borders, military doctrine
Commodity platforms (Meta/X/TikTok) dominate the attention layer
